title: Dog Calories by Breed & Age 2026 | Hacé Cuentas
h1: Calculate Your Dog's Daily Calorie Needs by Breed and Age
description: Calculate daily calories your dog needs by breed, weight, age & activity. Uses veterinary MER formula. Free & instant.
seoKeywords: how many calories does my dog need
intro: The **daily calories** your dog needs is calculated using the veterinary formula **MER (Maintenance Energy Requirement)**: **MER = 70 × (weight^0.75) × factor**, where the factor depends on age, activity level, neutering status, and body condition. This calculator applies the formula to each breed with its typical factor. The result helps you adjust food portions based on the kcal/100g listed on your food bag.
keyTakeaway: **RER** (resting) = 70 × (weight^0.75). **MER adult neutered**: RER × 1.6. **Puppy**: RER × 2–3. **Active**: RER × 2–5. Premium kibble has ~380 kcal/100g.
useCases: Convert kcal to grams of your specific dog food brand.
Your dog is overweight and you're calculating a calorie deficit.
Homemade diet: you need to know the daily calorie target.
Your dog is athletic (agility, canicross) and burns more calories.
Your vet asked for exact daily calorie needs.

explanation:
## MER Formula (Maintenance Energy Requirement)

```
RER = 70 × (weight_kg ^ 0.75)
MER = RER × factor
```

## Factors by Life Stage

| Life Stage | Factor |
|---|---|
| Puppy 2–4 months | 3.0 |
| Puppy 4–12 months | 2.0 |
| Adult intact active | 1.8 |
| Adult neutered | 1.6 |
| Adult sedentary (apartment, neutered) | 1.4 |
| Weight loss | 1.0 |
| Senior (>7 years) | 1.4 |
| Working / hunting / athletic | 2.0–5.0 |
| Pregnant (last third) | 3.0 |
| Lactating | 4–8 |

## Quick RER Reference Table

| Weight (kg) | RER (kcal) |
|---|---|
| 5 | 234 |
| 10 | 394 |
| 15 | 534 |
| 20 | 663 |
| 25 | 783 |
| 30 | 895 |
| 40 | 1,107 |
| 50 | 1,304 |

## Converting kcal to Grams of Dog Food

Check your food label (kcal/100g). Typical values:

- **Standard dry kibble**: 330 kcal/100g
- **Premium dry kibble**: 380 kcal/100g
- **Super premium dry kibble**: 410 kcal/100g
- **Wet/canned food**: 90–110 kcal/100g

- [Daily food portions by breed](/dog-daily-food-by-breed)
- [Ideal weight by breed](/ideal-dog-weight-by-breed)

faq.a: **MER** is the total daily energy your dog needs to maintain their current weight. It's calculated by multiplying the **RER** (Resting Energy Requirement) by a factor based on age, activity level, and neutering status.

faq.q: Why does the formula use 0.75 as an exponent?
faq.a: Because metabolic rate doesn't scale linearly with body weight—a dog twice as heavy doesn't burn twice as many calories. The 0.75 exponent (Kleiber's law, 1932) mathematically corrects for this biological relationship.

faq.q: How do I convert kcal to grams of dog food?
faq.a: Divide total daily kcal by the kcal per 100g listed on your food bag, then multiply by 100. Example: 1,200 kcal ÷ 380 kcal/100g × 100 = 316 g. Always check your food bag for the exact calorie content.

faq.q: How do I calculate calories for homemade dog meals?
faq.a: Add up the kcal of each ingredient (cooked chicken ~160 kcal/100g, cooked rice ~130, vegetables 40–60). **Important**: consult a veterinary nutritionist to ensure the diet is complete and balanced, not just calorie-correct.

faq.q: How much should I reduce my dog's calories for weight loss?
faq.a: Feed **70–80% of their MER** (a 20–30% calorie deficit). Target **1–2% of body weight lost per week**. Faster weight loss can be unsafe. Always check with your vet before starting a weight-loss diet.

faq.q: Do dog treats count toward daily calorie intake?
faq.a: **Yes, absolutely.** A typical treat has 5–20 kcal. Ten treats a day adds 50–200 kcal. Treats should not exceed **10% of your dog's total daily calories**.

faq.q: What's the difference between RER and MER?
faq.a: **RER (Resting Energy Requirement)** is the minimum calories your dog burns while resting. **MER (Maintenance Energy Requirement)** is the total daily calories needed after factoring in activity level. MER = RER × activity factor.

faq.q: What is a healthy weight for my dog?
faq.a: Ideal weight varies by breed and individual dog. Ask your vet to assess your dog's **Body Condition Score (BCS)**. A healthy dog should have a visible waist and ribs you can feel with light pressure (but not prominently see).
